items.q: How did Hitler come to power in 1933?
items.correct: He was legally appointed Chancellor by President Hindenburg as part of a conservative political deal, after the Nazis became the largest party in the Reichstag.
items.misconception: Hitler seized power through a violent coup or revolution.
items.random_wrong: Hitler won an outright majority in the 1932 presidential election.
items.why: The 1923 Beer Hall Putsch failed; in 1933 Hitler came to power constitutionally. Students confuse Nazi violence with the actual mechanism of takeover, which was legal manoeuvring.

items.q: Why did Hitler order the Night of the Long Knives in 1934?
items.correct: To eliminate Ernst Röhm and the SA leadership, reassure the army of his authority, and remove rivals — securing the army's loyalty when Hindenburg died weeks later.
items.misconception: Hitler purged the SA because they were Communists.
items.random_wrong: It was a battle in which the SA tried to overthrow Hitler.
items.why: The SA were Nazis, not Communists. Students miss that this was internal consolidation: Hitler traded the SA for army backing, completing the move to total power.

items.q: Why did the Treaty of Versailles cause resentment in Germany?
items.correct: It assigned sole war guilt to Germany (Article 231) and imposed harsh reparations, territorial losses and military restrictions.
items.misconception: Because Germany lost more soldiers than any other country in the war.
items.random_wrong: Because Germany was not invited to sign it.
items.why: Students conflate the harshness of the treaty with battlefield losses. Germany WAS excluded from negotiations, but the resentment was about the terms (especially the war-guilt clause) — not exclusion from signing.

items.q: Which Bolshevik slogan summarised Lenin's April Theses?
items.why: It's Peace, Land, Bread. “Workers of the world unite” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What was the immediate cause of the February Revolution?
items.why: It's Bread shortages in Petrograd. “German invasion” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did the Russian Civil War end?
items.why: The real figure is 1921 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
